personas.name: Engineering Manager
personas.headlineQuote: Help my team ship faster without making reliability worse.
personas.whoTheyAre: Engineering managers and directors at mid-market software companies (50-1000 employees). They run teams of 5-30 engineers, report into a VP or CTO, and own delivery for one or more product areas. Their day is split between unblocking engineers, attending cross-functional planning, and reporting upward on velocity + incident response. They didn't become EMs to babysit dashboards; they became EMs to ship.
characteristics: Promoted from senior IC within the last 2-4 years; still codes occasionally.
Owns 1-3 squads. Has a lead/staff IC under them on each.
On call rotation regularly, including for non-team services they depend on.
Carries a quarterly OKR for team velocity + a quarterly OKR for reliability (often in tension).
Reads HackerNews / Lenny's Newsletter / The Pragmatic Engineer; attends 1-2 conferences a year.
Will adopt a tool if it removes ongoing toil from their team; will not adopt a tool that adds process they have to enforce.
jobsToBeDone: Reduce the number of production incidents the team is dragged into.
Cut MTTR (mean time to recovery) without adding more on-call rotations.
Give engineers self-serve access to the data they need without filing a ticket to ops.
Demonstrate quarterly improvement on a tangible reliability metric to the VP.
Onboard new hires faster by making the system self-describing.
valueProps: One pane of glass across logs, metrics, traces - your engineers stop hopping tools mid-incident.
OOTB dashboards for the SLIs that actually matter (latency, error rate, saturation) - configurable, not from-scratch.
Per-service ownership baked into alerting routes - the right team gets paged, every time.
Pricing scales with active hosts, not data volume - your bill is predictable as you grow.
painPoints: Datadog bill is growing 30% faster than the team. Finance is asking questions.
Half the incidents start with 'I can't find the log.' Then 20 minutes of tool-hopping.
Alerts fire to the wrong team. The first 10 minutes of every incident is figuring out who owns what.
The runbook says 'check Grafana' but Grafana shows 14 dashboards and 6 of them are stale.
New hires take 4+ weeks to be productive because the observability story isn't legible.
desiredOutcomes: Quarterly incident count trending down without quarterly headcount adds.
Service ownership in one place, queryable, and the source of truth for alert routing.
Observability bill flat or improving as throughput grows.
New hire ramp from PR-merged to incident-leading in under 3 weeks.
objections: We just paid for Datadog renewal. We can't switch right now.
Our SREs built our current stack. We can't be seen to be replacing their work.
Migrations are scary. Our last observability migration took 9 months and morale tanked.
Procurement is a 6-month process here. We'd love to but we can't move fast.

personas.name: VP Engineering
personas.headlineQuote: Infrastructure that scales with the business, not against it.
personas.whoTheyAre: VPs and SVPs of Engineering at scaleups and mid-market software companies (100+ employees, often Series B-D). They own multi-team engineering strategy + cross-functional partnerships with Product and Sales. They report to the CTO or CEO. Buying authority on infrastructure tools that affect more than one team. They're 3-5 years past being a hands-on EM; they think in headcount-and-tools tradeoffs.
characteristics: Spends most of the day in meetings: 1:1s with EMs, exec staff, partnerships with Product/Sales.
Carries the quarterly engineering plan, the hiring plan, the tools budget.
Has either a board-level OKR on engineering velocity or a board-level OKR on production reliability.
Considers vendor adoption as much about org change as about technology.
Will say no to anything that adds a fixed cost without a clear ROI story they can take to the CFO.
jobsToBeDone: Hit the quarterly delivery commitments without burning out the engineering org.
Build a reliability story that's defensible to the board after the next incident.
Consolidate the tool sprawl that came from years of bottom-up adoption.
Make the engineering org's productivity legible to non-technical stakeholders.
Avoid the next major incident having a 'should have caught this in observability' postmortem line.
valueProps: ROI story that maps directly to incident cost reduction + observability tool consolidation.
Org-level reliability dashboards your CTO can take to the board, not just team-level service dashboards.
Replace 3-4 tools (logs, metrics, traces, RUM) with one, in stages, without a forklift migration.
Procurement-friendly: predictable pricing, SOC 2, EU data residency available.
painPoints: Engineering tools budget is up 80% YoY. The CFO is asking what 'cost of complexity' is.
Last quarter's reliability metrics looked fine right up until the Tuesday outage; the board wants explanations.
Three teams use three different tracing tools because they bought independently before tools-governance kicked in.
Engineering hiring is slowing - need to do more with the team we have, but the toil is going the wrong direction.
desiredOutcomes: Single observability vendor across all teams within 12 months.
Engineering tools budget growth flat or below headcount growth.
Director-level EMs reporting tool-related friction as a top-3 blocker decline below 10%.
objections: We already have Datadog. The cost to switch outweighs the marginal improvement.
Our infra team is small; another migration project is not realistic this year.
We can't have a vendor change be the headline of next quarter's eng review.
Show me the ROI in writing before I add another vendor to our procurement queue.

personas.name: Platform Engineer
personas.headlineQuote: Cut the toil. Make the platform feel native.
personas.whoTheyAre: Individual contributors on platform / SRE / infrastructure teams at scaleups and mid-market companies. They own the developer platform, the build/deploy pipelines, the on-call rotation for shared services, and observability tooling. They're senior ICs (often staff-level or close to it). They're influential on tool selection - their EM will give weight to their recommendations - but they're not the final buyer.
characteristics: 5+ years of experience; deeply technical and opinionated.
Owns the on-call rotation for the platform + shared services.
Has scars from at least one tool migration that went badly.
Will read the docs before talking to sales. Will silently dismiss any tool whose docs are bad.
Adopts via 'install and try it on a side service' before committing.
Skeptical of marketing language; persuaded by concrete examples, query syntax, pricing pages, status pages.
jobsToBeDone: Reduce the time it takes to debug a production issue.
Eliminate the manual instrumentation toil engineers complain about.
Get to per-service ownership data without manually maintaining a spreadsheet.
Build internal documentation that doesn't go stale (because the tool keeps it current).
Make on-call rotations less painful for the team.
valueProps: Auto-instrumentation: drop the agent in, get traces for the services you didn't touch.
Query language that doesn't make you context-switch from your IDE to learn it.
Per-service ownership pulled from your repo or your service catalogue, not manually edited.
Self-hostable for regulated workloads; managed for everything else.
painPoints: Half the services don't have tracing because someone has to manually instrument them.
The service catalogue is a spreadsheet that goes stale within 2 sprints.
Building a meaningful dashboard takes 4 hours - and no one updates them after launch.
Datadog queries don't compose with my logs queries; I'm cutting and pasting between tabs.
On-call quality is dragging on team morale; turnover risk on the platform team.
desiredOutcomes: All services traced by default within one quarter, including the ones nobody owns.
Service ownership data correct enough that auto-generated alerts route correctly.
Dashboard authoring time cut from hours to minutes.
On-call survey scores climb back above 4/5.
Less time playing detective during incidents.
objections: I've been burned by the 'we'll auto-instrument everything' promise before.
Show me the query language. Don't tell me you have one.
Our existing stack has 18 months of muscle memory. I need a clear migration story.
Pricing pages: if I can't model the bill from my actual data volumes, I'm not even bringing it to my manager.
